prompt: A vibe-coded function compiles, runs without error, and reads like clean idiomatic code, but returns the wrong total for a negative-discount edge case. Which failure modes does this illustrate, and what reliably contains them? Select ALL that apply.
choices: It is plausible-but-broken: fluent, confident code that mishandles an edge case
It is silent wrong code: it runs and returns a value, but the value is wrong
Reading the diff against the spec's edge cases plus a behavioural test reliably catches it
Confirming 'it runs without crashing' is sufficient to rule the code correct

prompt: Why is each vibe-coding discipline best understood as a defense against a specific failure mode rather than a generic ritual? Select ALL that apply.
choices: Behavioural tests target silent wrong code, while reading the diff targets plausible-but-broken logic
Small, single-purpose diffs isolate scope creep, and the 'never ship what you don't understand' rule contains eroded understanding
The disciplines compose like layered defenses, so dropping one lets the failure it contained walk through
Any one discipline alone guarantees no AI-generated bug can ever reach production

prompt: An architect reviews a sprint where the team accepted many large, unread AI diffs to move fast, and now no one can fully explain how a core module works. What is the right way to frame and address this?
choices: This is eroded understanding accumulating as risk; restore the mental model by reading, testing, and refactoring toward coherence
This is ideal velocity; keep accepting unread diffs as long as the app still starts
This is purely a tooling problem; switch AI models and the understanding returns
This is harmless because version control means the code can always be reverted later
